Notes: First petawatt laser; paper reports 1.5 PW.
Description: Nova Petawatt was an Nd:glass CPA laser created by converting one beamline of the Nova facility at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory. It started operation in 1996 and served as the first major demonstration that existing high-energy fusion-laser infrastructure could be adapted for ultra-short-pulse experiments.

Notes: First PW-class user facility.
Description: Vulcan Petawatt is an Nd:glass CPA beamline at the Central Laser Facility at Rutherford Appleton Laboratory in the UK. It began petawatt user-facility operation in 2004 and supports high-energy-density and laser-plasma experiments synchronized with long-pulse Vulcan beams.

Notes: Paper reports 2.1 kJ at 10 ps and “almost 1 PW” at best compression.
Description: OMEGA-EP is a four-beam extension of the OMEGA laser at the Laboratory for Laser Energetics at the University of Rochester. It started operation in 2008 and provides short-pulse and long-pulse capabilities for radiography, proton generation, and inertial-confinement-fusion experiments.

Notes: Multi-beam long-pulse petawatt-class facility.
Description: NIF is the National Ignition Facility at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, a large multi-beam Nd:glass laser designed for inertial confinement fusion and high-energy-density physics. It was dedicated in 2009 and operates as a megajoule-scale facility with many synchronized beamlines.

Notes: High-average-power PW system.
Description: HAPLS / L3 is a high-average-power Ti:sapphire laser developed by Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory for ELI-Beamlines in the Czech Republic. It was commissioned in 2018 and is designed for high-repetition-rate petawatt-class operation using diode-pumped solid-state pump lasers.

Notes: World’s first PW-class Ti:sapphire facility.
Description: J-KAREN is a Ti:sapphire laser at QST/KPSI in Japan developed for relativistic laser-plasma interaction studies. It began petawatt-class operation in 2003 and later evolved into the upgraded J-KAREN-P system with improved contrast and stability.

Notes: First 1 Hz petawatt laser.
Description: BELLA is the Berkeley Lab Laser Accelerator facility at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory. It started operation in 2013 and was built specifically to drive laser-plasma acceleration experiments with stable high-repetition-rate operation.

Notes: High rep-rate PW-class Ti:sapphire system.
Description: CSU Advanced Beam Laboratory is a Ti:sapphire high-intensity laser facility at Colorado State University. Its petawatt-class operation was reported in 2017, and the facility uses a high-repetition-rate pump-laser architecture to support frequent laser-plasma experiments.

Notes: Also operates 100 TW at 10 Hz.
Description: Diocles is a Ti:sapphire laser facility at the Extreme Light Laboratory at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln. It came online in 2008 and was later upgraded in 2012 to support higher-power operation alongside separate high-repetition-rate beamlines.

Laser technology: OPCPA front end + mixed Nd:glass CPA
Notes: Paper reports 1.1 PW.
Description: Texas Petawatt is a high-energy short-pulse laser at the University of Texas at Austin. It began operation around 2010 and combines an OPCPA front end with mixed Nd:glass amplification to support high-intensity laser-plasma experiments.

Notes: 15/40 gives 0.375 PW; paper reports 400 TW.
Description: Scarlet is a Ti:sapphire dual-CPA laser at Ohio State University. It became operational in 2012 and was built for relativistic plasma physics, ion acceleration, and high-energy-density experiments in a dedicated controlled laboratory environment.

Notes: Paper reports 200 TW original, 500 TW upgraded.
Description: ALLS is the Advanced Laser Light Source at INRS in Quebec, Canada. It began operation in the late 2000s and uses a commercial Ti:sapphire system for ultrafast high-intensity experiments, later receiving an upgrade to higher capability.

Notes: Two synchronized 500 TW beams.
Description: Gemini is a two-beam Ti:sapphire laser facility at the Central Laser Facility at Rutherford Appleton Laboratory. It began operation as a user facility in 2009 and provides synchronized short-pulse beams for laser-plasma acceleration and high-intensity interaction studies.

Notes: Also has 400 TW green high-contrast mode.
Description: Orion is a laser facility at AWE Aldermaston in the UK combining long-pulse Nd:glass beams with synchronized short-pulse beams. It became operational in 2013 and supports high-energy-density physics with both infrared and high-contrast frequency-doubled operating modes.

Reference DOI/link: Facilities – Scottish Centre for the Application of Plasma-based Accelerators
Notes: Europe’s highest average-power commercial petawatt-scale laser at paper time.
Description: SCAPA is the Scottish Centre for the Application of Plasma-based Accelerators at the University of Strathclyde. Its commercial Ti:sapphire laser was commissioned in 2017 and supports multiple laser-plasma accelerator beamlines for electron, proton, ion, and radiation-source research.

Notes: Multi-kJ design, synchronized to LMJ.
Description: PETAL is the short-pulse beamline coupled to the Laser Megajoule facility at CEA in France. It was initially commissioned in 2015 and provides synchronized high-energy short-pulse capability for academic and high-energy-density experiments alongside LMJ.

Notes: Switchable long/short pulse glass system.
Description: PHELIX is uniquely integrated with GSI's heavy-ion accelerator complex, enabling synchronized laser–ion pump–probe experiments for investigations of warm dense matter, plasma physics, and matter under extreme conditions. The facility supports laser-driven secondary source development—including protons, electrons, neutrons, and X-rays—and has played a significant role in advancing high-energy-density physics through combined laser and accelerator capabilities.

Notes: Compressor limits on-target power.
Description: POLARIS is distinguished as one of the world's first fully diode-pumped, high-power ultrafast laser systems, demonstrating the scalability and efficiency of diode-pumped petawatt-class technology. The facility primarily supports research in laser-driven particle acceleration, relativistic laser–plasma interactions, and the development of next-generation high-repetition-rate high-intensity laser architectures.

Notes: Also 150 TW at 10 Hz.
Description: DRACO (Dresden Laser Acceleration Source) is a user facility dedicated to relativistic laser–plasma interaction research, with particular emphasis on laser-wakefield electron acceleration, ion acceleration, and secondary radiation source generation. Its close integration with HZDR's ELBE accelerator infrastructure enables advanced beam diagnostics and studies that bridge conventional accelerator physics with plasma-based acceleration techniques.

Notes: Energy/duration gives 0.33 PW.
Description: JETI200 is optimized for ultrashort-pulse experiments requiring exceptional temporal contrast, achieved through a double chirped-pulse amplification architecture with cross-polarized wave generation. The system serves as a versatile platform for studies of relativistic laser–matter interactions, laser-driven electron and ion acceleration, and high-harmonic generation, while also supporting the development of advanced target and diagnostic technologies.

Notes: Three-line VEGA system; VEGA-3 is PW line.
Description: VEGA-3 is the flagship beamline of Spain's VEGA laser system and forms part of a synchronized multi-beam architecture that enables flexible pump–probe and multi-pulse experimental configurations. As an international user facility, it supports research in laser-driven particle acceleration, secondary radiation sources, warm dense matter, and high-intensity laser–plasma physics, while emphasizing high repetition rate operation and broad experimental accessibility.

Notes: Also 45 TW at 10 Hz mode.
Description: CETAL (Center for Advanced Laser Technologies) was established as Romania's national petawatt-class laser facility to support research in high-field laser physics, laser–plasma interactions, advanced photonics, and materials science. In addition to serving as a platform for fundamental research, the facility provides a technological foundation for developing high-power laser instrumentation and complements the larger European-scale capabilities available at ELI-NP.

Notes: Paper cites March 2019 10 PW demonstration.
Description: ELI-NP is the world's first operational dual-arm 10 PW laser facility, designed specifically to enable experiments at the intersection of ultra-intense laser science and nuclear physics. By combining two synchronized 10 PW beamlines with a high-brilliance gamma-beam source, the facility supports frontier research in laser-driven nuclear reactions, nonlinear quantum electrodynamics, particle acceleration, and laboratory astrophysics, while operating as an international user facility.

Notes: First operational PW-class OPCPA path.
Description: PEARL (PEtawatt pARametric Laser) pioneered the use of optical parametric chirped-pulse amplification (OPCPA) for generating high-energy ultrashort pulses, demonstrating an alternative to conventional Ti:sapphire petawatt architectures. The facility has served as a testbed for high-contrast pulse generation and investigations of relativistic laser–matter interactions, while informing the development of subsequent multi-petawatt OPCPA systems worldwide.

Notes: Designed for 5 PW.
Description: The SG-II 5 PW OPCPA system was developed to demonstrate the scalability of optical parametric chirped-pulse amplification to multi-petawatt peak powers while maintaining excellent pulse contrast and broad amplification bandwidth. The facility supports research in relativistic laser–plasma interactions, laboratory astrophysics, high-energy-density physics, and strong-field quantum electrodynamics, and has served as a key technology demonstrator for China's next generation of ultra-intense laser facilities.

Notes: Earlier 0.89 PW in 2006.
Description: Qiangguang was developed to investigate relativistic laser–plasma interactions and the generation of laser-driven particle and radiation sources at ultra-high intensities. The facility has also served as a testbed for advancing high-field laser technologies, including improvements in pulse contrast, beam quality, and target interaction physics that support next-generation ultra-intense laser systems.

Notes: Paper reports improvement to 1 PW.
Description: This hybrid laser architecture combines conventional chirped-pulse amplification (CPA) with optical parametric chirped-pulse amplification (OPCPA) to leverage the high energy scalability of CPA and the broad bandwidth and high temporal contrast of OPCPA. The system was developed as a technology demonstrator for future multi-petawatt lasers, validating hybrid amplification strategies that have since been incorporated into higher-power facilities such as SULF.

Notes: Full SULF aimed at 10 PW.
Description: The SULF prototype served as the precursor to the full Shanghai Superintense Ultrafast Laser Facility, demonstrating key technologies required for reliable 10 PW-class operation, including high-energy Ti:sapphire amplification, pulse compression, and ultrahigh temporal contrast. Its successful commissioning established the technological foundation for the operational SULF user facility, which supports research in strong-field physics, high-energy-density science, particle acceleration, and ultrafast materials science.

Notes: Planned scaling to ~15 PW.
Description: CAEP-PW is a multi-petawatt laser facility developed to investigate relativistic laser–matter interactions, laboratory astrophysics, and high-energy-density physics under extreme electromagnetic fields. The system emphasizes high pulse quality and temporal contrast, providing a platform for experiments in laser-driven particle acceleration, secondary radiation source generation, and strong-field plasma physics while contributing to the development of China's next generation of ultra-intense laser technology.

Notes: First >1 PW facility in China.
Description: XL-III is a petawatt-class Ti:sapphire laser facility designed for studies of ultrafast, relativistic laser–plasma interactions and the production of high-brightness secondary particle and photon sources. The facility has been extensively used to investigate laser-driven electron and ion acceleration, high-order harmonic generation, and nonlinear strong-field phenomena, while also serving as a platform for developing advanced diagnostics and high-intensity laser techniques.

Notes: Paper gives 0.7 PW and duration; energy estimated.
Description: The XG-III femtosecond beam is an ultrashort-pulse beamline within the XingGuang-III laser platform, designed to support pump–probe experiments and relativistic laser–plasma interaction studies relevant to inertial confinement fusion and high-energy-density physics. Its integration with long-pulse capabilities enables time-resolved investigations of matter under extreme conditions, providing a versatile platform for laser-driven particle acceleration and secondary radiation source generation.

Notes: First petawatt class laser in Asia.
Description: The petawatt beam added to the GEKKO XII laser facility was developed to investigate the fast-ignition approach to inertial confinement fusion by combining ultrashort, ultra-intense heating pulses with long-pulse target compression. Beyond fusion research, the beamline has enabled studies in relativistic laser–plasma interactions, high-energy-density physics, and laser-driven particle acceleration, establishing GEKKO XII as one of the pioneering hybrid long- and short-pulse laser facilities.

Laser technology: Four-beam segmented Nd:glass CPA with OPCPA front end
Notes: Paper reports current 2 PW in 1 ps.
Description: LFEX was specifically developed to deliver multi-kilojoule, picosecond laser pulses for fast-ignition inertial confinement fusion experiments in conjunction with the GEKKO XII compression laser. In addition to advancing fast-ignition research, the facility supports investigations of relativistic plasma physics, high-energy-density science, and intense laser–matter interactions at extreme energy densities.

Notes: High-contrast PW-class upgrade.
Description: J-KAREN-P is a high-contrast petawatt Ti:sapphire laser optimized for experiments requiring exceptional pulse fidelity, making it particularly well suited for relativistic laser–plasma interactions, laser-driven ion and electron acceleration, and high-field quantum electrodynamics. The facility has been widely used to develop advanced target technologies and precision diagnostics, while serving as one of Japan's flagship user facilities for ultra-intense laser science.

Notes: Two 0.5 PW beams; user mode initially reduced.
Description: The HERMES laser is an ultrafast optical laser system synchronized with the SACLA X-ray free-electron laser (XFEL), enabling femtosecond pump–probe experiments that combine ultra-intense optical excitation with coherent hard X-ray diagnostics. This integrated platform supports investigations of ultrafast structural dynamics, high-field light–matter interactions, nonlinear X-ray physics, and strong-field quantum electrodynamics, leveraging the exceptional temporal synchronization between the optical and XFEL beamlines.

Laser technology: Ti:sapphire CPA with XPW/OPA front end
Notes: Upgraded from earlier 1.1 PW and 1.5 PW beamlines.
Description: The CoReLS 4 PW laser was developed to achieve extreme focused intensities through advanced wavefront correction and adaptive optics, becoming the first laser system to experimentally exceed an intensity of 1 × 10^23 W/cm^2. The facility is a leading platform for strong-field quantum electrodynamics, relativistic laser-plasma interactions, laser-driven particle acceleration, and laboratory astrophysics, with ongoing research into nonlinear Compton scattering, Breit-Wheeler pair production, and radiation-pressure ion acceleration.

Notes: Paper calls it upgraded to 1 PW.
Description: EXLS is a conceptual exawatt-class laser architecture proposed to investigate physical regimes beyond those accessible with current multi-petawatt facilities. The design explores coherent beam combination and advanced pulse compression technologies to enable experiments in nonlinear quantum electrodynamics, vacuum pair production, laboratory astrophysics, and other extreme-field phenomena anticipated at exawatt intensities.

Notes: Paper also notes existing 150 TW, 5 Hz system.
Description: The RRCAT petawatt laser was developed as India's flagship ultrafast high-intensity laser facility to support research in relativistic laser–matter interactions, laser-driven particle acceleration, high-energy-density physics, and secondary radiation source generation. In addition to enabling fundamental studies in strong-field science, the facility provides a national platform for advancing indigenous high-power laser technologies and ultrafast diagnostics.

Notes: Demonstrated 105 mJ, 10.4 fs pulses at 1.7 µm and 10 Hz, making it a strong high-energy shortwave-infrared dual-chirped OPA source.
Description: This system is a high-peak-power dual-chirped optical parametric amplifier operating near 1.7 µm, designed to generate few-cycle infrared pulses. Its significance is that it pushes sub-two-cycle pulse generation into the 100-mJ energy class, making it useful for strong-field physics and long-wavelength high-harmonic generation.

Notes: Generated 30 mJ, 11.6 fs two-cycle pulses at 1.8 µm using OPCPA and frequency-domain synthesis for attosecond and strong-field applications.
Description: This OPCPA system produces few-cycle pulses near 1.8 µm using frequency-domain synthesis and optical parametric amplification. It is important because it provides high-power, carrier-envelope-phase-stable pulses in the short-wave infrared region for attosecond science and nonlinear optics.

Institution: ETH Zürich, Ultrafast Laser Physics group
Notes: Produced 250 µJ, 16.5 fs pulses at 2.2 µm with a 100 kHz repetition rate, emphasizing high-average-power mid-IR operation rather than extreme peak power.
Description: This laser is a high-repetition-rate OPCPA source operating near 2.2 µm, emphasizing average power and stability rather than maximum single-shot energy. Its high repetition rate makes it valuable for high-harmonic generation, water-window X-ray production, and precision ultrafast spectroscopy.

Notes: Advanced DC-OPA system delivering 53 mJ, 8.58 fs pulses at 2.44 µm and 10 Hz, giving multi-terawatt few-cycle SWIR output.
Description: This advanced dual-chirped OPA operates near 2.44 µm and reaches multi-terawatt peak power with near-single-cycle pulse duration. It is significant because it extends high-energy, ultrashort infrared pulse generation deeper into the mid-infrared regime.

Institution: Shanghai Jiao Tong University-related mid-IR group
Notes: Tunable 3–5 µm noncollinear OPA source with a representative 3.425 µm point, delivering 13.3 mJ in 111 fs at 10 Hz.
Description: This tunable mid-infrared OPA covers a broad wavelength range from roughly 3 to 5 µm while maintaining high pulse energy and short pulse duration. It is useful as a flexible source for wavelength-dependent strong-field physics, molecular spectroscopy, and mid-IR nonlinear optics.

Laser technology: KTA-based OPCPA + krypton-filled hollow-core fiber compression
Notes: KTA-based 4 µm OPCPA followed by krypton-filled hollow-core-fiber compression produced 2.6 mJ, 21.5 fs pulses at 100 Hz.
Description: This system combines a 4 µm OPCPA with hollow-core fiber compression to produce near-single-cycle mid-infrared pulses. Its importance comes from achieving high peak power at 4 µm, a wavelength region useful for driving long-wavelength high-harmonic generation and relativistic laser–matter studies.

Institution: Max Born Institute for Nonlinear Optics and Short Pulse Spectroscopy
Notes: ZnGeP2 OPCPA at about 4.9 µm delivered 3.4 mJ, 89.4 fs pulses at 1 kHz, extending high-repetition OPCPA into the mid-IR.
Description: This ZnGeP₂-based OPCPA system generates high-energy ultrashort pulses around 5 µm, reaching record-class peak power beyond the common 3–4 µm mid-IR range. It is important because 5 µm sources are technically difficult and useful for molecular fingerprint spectroscopy and long-wavelength strong-field experiments.

Notes: Ho:YLF-pumped OPCPA produced 0.75 mJ, 188 fs pulses near 7 µm at 100 Hz, providing a long-wavelength mid-IR data point.
Description: This 7 µm OPCPA extends high-peak-power ultrafast laser operation into the long-wave mid-infrared region. It is significant because few systems can generate sub-picosecond, millijoule-class pulses at this wavelength, making it a valuable anchor point for peak-power versus wavelength comparisons.

Institution: Brookhaven National Laboratory Accelerator Test Facility
Reference DOI/link: BNL | Accelerator Test Facility | CO2 Laser
Notes: BNL ATF CO2 CPA is a long-wavelength gas-laser facility capability around 9.2 µm, designed for joule-class picosecond CO2 laser experiments.
Description: The BNL ATF CO₂ CPA laser is a long-wave infrared laser operating near 9.2 µm with terawatt-class peak power. It is especially important for laser wakefield acceleration and inverse-Compton scattering experiments because CO₂ lasers naturally match plasma and accelerator physics applications at longer wavelengths.

Notes: UCLA Neptune demonstrated a high-energy CO2 MOPA laser near 10.6 µm with 45 J, 3 ps pulses, giving a strong LWIR peak-power point.
Description: The UCLA Neptune CO₂ laser is a high-peak-power 10.6 µm CO₂ system that demonstrated multi-terawatt picosecond pulses. It is an important historical LWIR benchmark because it showed that gas-based CO₂ laser systems could reach peak powers relevant to plasma acceleration and high-field laser–matter interaction studies.

Institution: Max Born Institute for Nonlinear Optics and Short Pulse Spectroscopy
Notes: GaSe-based LWIR OPCPA reached 65 µJ, 185 fs pulses at 11.4 µm and 1 kHz, extending OPCPA coverage beyond 10 µm.
Description: This LWIR OPCPA system operating at 11.4 µm extends ultrafast optical parametric amplification into the long-wave infrared region using GaSe nonlinear crystals. It demonstrates one of the longest-wavelength femtosecond OPCPA sources reported, enabling strong-field physics, molecular spectroscopy, and long-wavelength nonlinear optics.

Laser/facility: Ho:YLF CPA pump laser for MIR OPCPA
Notes: Ho:YLF CPA system at 2.051 µm delivered 28 mJ, 6.8 ps pulses at 1 kHz as a pump platform for mid-IR OPCPA.
Description: This Ho:YLF chirped-pulse amplification laser serves as a high-energy pump source for mid-infrared OPCPA systems. Its high pulse energy and excellent stability make it a key enabling technology for scaling high-peak-power lasers beyond the traditional Ti:sapphire wavelength range.

Reference DOI/link: Experiments and numerical simulations of Ho:CALGO regenerative amplifier at 2.08 µm
Notes: Ho:CALGO CPA plus nonlinear compression produced sub-100 fs pulses near 2.1 µm at 100 kHz, useful as a high-repetition SWIR amplifier example.
Description: This Ho:CALGO regenerative amplifier generates high-average-power femtosecond pulses near 2.1 µm with excellent beam quality. It is designed as an efficient seed source for next-generation mid-infrared OPCPA systems and high-repetition-rate strong-field experiments.

Notes: KALDERA is a planned diode-pumped Yb:YAG CPA system targeting 3 J, 30 fs, 100 TW pulses at 1 kHz for high-average-power laser-plasma applications.
Description: KALDERA is a diode-pumped Yb:YAG chirped-pulse amplification laser being developed to deliver high-average-power femtosecond pulses at kilohertz repetition rates. The project targets applications requiring both high peak power and high throughput, including plasma accelerators and advanced photon sources.

Notes: MEC-U is a design-stage DPSSL glass CPA system aiming for 150 J, 150 fs, 1 PW pulses at 10 Hz for SLAC high-energy-density experiments.
Description: MEC-U is the next-generation Matter in Extreme Conditions laser at SLAC, designed to provide petawatt-class pulses at significantly higher repetition rates than conventional flashlamp-pumped systems. It will support ultrafast X-ray science, high-energy-density physics, and laser-plasma interaction experiments alongside the Linac Coherent Light Source.

Reference DOI/link: High power (>5 W) λ∼9.6 μm tapered quantum cascade lasers grown by OMVPE - ScienceDirect
Notes: ZGP OPCPA near 8.9 µm is listed as a low-energy ultrafast LWIR source, but the source link and exact parameters should be rechecked before final use.
Description: This ZnGeP₂ OPCPA system operating near 8.9 µm demonstrates one of the highest-power ultrafast laser sources in the long-wave mid-infrared. It extends nonlinear optical amplification toward the CO₂ laser wavelength region while maintaining femtosecond pulse durations.

Notes: ICFO’s Ho:YLF-pumped ZGP OPCPA produced 0.75 mJ, 188 fs pulses near 6.8 µm at 100 Hz, overlapping with the 7 µm OPCPA entry.
Description: This OPCPA system generates ultrashort pulses near 6.8 µm using a Ho:YLF-pumped optical parametric architecture. It bridges the wavelength gap between conventional mid-infrared OPCPA systems and long-wave infrared laser technologies, supporting molecular spectroscopy and high-field applications.

Institution: Ludwig Maximilian University (LMU Munich) / Max Planck Institute of Quantum Optics (MPQ)
Notes: LMU/MPQ OPCPA is listed as a few-cycle 810 nm, 4.4 TW source, but the listed 600 nJ energy does not match the stated peak power and should be verified.
Description: This 2.1 µm OPCPA system delivers 44 TW peak power while maintaining few-cycle pulse durations and high beam quality. It represents one of the most powerful ultrafast laser systems operating near the 2 µm wavelength and demonstrates the scalability of OPCPA technology beyond Ti:sapphire lasers.

Notes: IST’s 3.0 µm OPCPA delivered multi-mJ, 140 fs pulses at 100 Hz, providing a mid-IR source optimized for strong-field experiments.
Description: This multi-millijoule OPCPA operating at 3 µm provides high pulse energy with femtosecond pulse durations for strong-field mid-infrared research. It serves as an important platform for high-harmonic generation, nonlinear optics, and laser-driven particle acceleration studies.

Institution: Max Born Institute for Nonlinear Optics and Short Pulse Spectroscopy
Notes: MBI’s ZGP OPCPA produced 0.65 mJ, 75 fs pulses around 5.1 µm at 1 kHz, demonstrating high-repetition mid-IR few-cycle amplification.
Description: This ZnGeP₂-based OPCPA system demonstrated one of the earliest high-energy femtosecond laser sources beyond 5 µm. It established the feasibility of scaling ultrafast mid-infrared lasers toward longer wavelengths while maintaining high peak power and excellent pulse quality.

Institution: Shanghai Institute of Optics and Fine Mechanics (SIOM) / ShanghaiTech University
Notes: SULF is a SIOM/ShanghaiTech Ti:sapphire CPA facility targeting 10+ PW operation and listed here with a 12.8 PW, 808 nm, 339 J short-pulse capability.
Description: SULF is one of the world's most powerful Ti:sapphire laser facilities, achieving a demonstrated peak power of 12.8 PW. Developed by the Shanghai Institute of Optics and Fine Mechanics (SIOM), it serves as a flagship user facility for relativistic laser–plasma physics, quantum electrodynamics (QED), particle acceleration, and high-energy-density science.

Reference DOI/link: World's most powerful laser, developed by Thales and ELI-NP, achieves record power level of 10 PW | Laser Focus World
Notes: ELI-NP’s 10 PW Ti:sapphire CPA beamline demonstrated 250 J, 25 fs pulses near 820 nm, making it one of the benchmark multi-petawatt laser facilities.
Description: The ELI-NP High Power Laser System is the first operational user facility capable of delivering 10 PW laser pulses. Located in Romania, it combines two independent 10 PW beamlines with a gamma-beam facility to support nuclear physics, laser-driven particle acceleration, photonuclear experiments, and studies of matter under extreme electromagnetic fields.

Reference DOI/link: The US has a new most powerful laser - Michigan Engineering News
Notes: ZEUS at the University of Michigan is listed as a 2 PW, 800 nm Ti:sapphire CPA system designed to provide high-intensity laser access in the United States.
Description: ZEUS (Zettawatt-Equivalent Ultrashort pulse laser System) is the newest flagship laser facility at the University of Michigan and currently the highest peak-power laser operating in the United States. Its 2 PW beamline is designed for laser wakefield acceleration, high-energy-density physics, and experiments reaching zettawatt-equivalent intensities in the electron rest frame.

Reference DOI/link: The 2 PW/1 Hz laser system for laser-driven proton tumor therapy facility CLAPA-II: Compact laser plasma accelerator II.
Notes: CLAPA-II is a commissioning-stage 2 PW Ti:sapphire CPA system intended for laser-driven proton therapy and compact accelerator research.
Description: CLAPA-II (Compact Laser Plasma Accelerator II) is a next-generation 2 PW Ti:sapphire laser facility being developed at Peking University for laser-driven proton acceleration and medical accelerator research. Its primary goal is to advance compact proton sources suitable for future cancer therapy while supporting high-intensity laser–plasma experiments.

Notes: LAP at Tsinghua/Tsung-Dao Lee Institute is listed as a commissioning-stage 3 PW Ti:sapphire CPA system for high-intensity laser-plasma studies.
Description: The Laser for Astrophysics and Plasma Physics (LAP) is a multi-petawatt laser being constructed at the Tsung-Dao Lee Institute of Shanghai Jiao Tong University. It is designed to investigate laboratory astrophysics, relativistic plasma physics, and extreme laser–matter interactions at the frontier of high-field science.

Notes: SECUF is a commissioning-stage 1 PW, 1 Hz Ti:sapphire CPA facility aimed at high-repetition ultrafast strong-field and plasma experiments.
Description: SECUF (Station of Extreme Conditions User Facility) is a 1 PW Ti:sapphire laser platform developed by the Chinese Academy of Sciences to provide open access for high-field scientific research. The facility emphasizes reliable user operation and supports studies in plasma physics, ultrafast dynamics, particle acceleration, and secondary radiation generation.

Institution: Raja Ramanna Centre for Advanced Technology (RRCAT), Indore
Notes: RRCAT PW is India’s commissioning/design petawatt Ti:sapphire CPA system targeting 25 J, 25 fs pulses for laser-plasma acceleration research.
Description: The Raja Ramanna Centre for Advanced Technology (RRCAT) Petawatt Laser is India's first indigenous petawatt-class Ti:sapphire laser system. It is being commissioned for laser wakefield acceleration, relativistic laser–plasma interactions, and advanced accelerator research, representing a major milestone in India's high-power laser program.

Institution: University of Rochester, Laboratory for Laser Energetics
Laser technology: All-OPCPA; DKDP amplifiers; OMEGA EP-coupled concept
Notes: NSF OPAL/EP-OPAL is a design/prototyping all-OPCPA concept at LLE aiming for 25 PW-class pulses coupled to OMEGA EP capabilities.
Description: OPAL (Optical Petawatt Laser) is a proposed next-generation U.S. user facility being developed at the University of Rochester's Laboratory for Laser Energetics. The project aims to deliver two 25 PW all-OPCPA beamlines to support high-energy-density physics, laboratory astrophysics, inertial fusion science, and strong-field quantum electrodynamics experiments.

Institution: Central Laser Facility, STFC Rutherford Appleton Laboratory
Reference DOI/link: Mace | Building one of the world’s most powerful lasers
Notes: Vulcan 20-20 is a planned Central Laser Facility OPCPA upgrade targeting 20 PW pulses from 400 J in 20 fs at a low design repetition rate.
Description: Vulcan 20-20 is the major upgrade of the UK's Vulcan laser at the Central Laser Facility, increasing its capability from petawatt to approximately 20 PW operation. The upgrade emphasizes both extremely high peak power and improved experimental flexibility for particle acceleration, high-energy-density physics, fusion science, and laboratory astrophysics.

Laser technology: Mixed Nd:glass / OPCPA-style high-energy CPA architecture
Reference DOI/link: ELI’s L4 ATON Laser Reaches 5 Petawatt Output | Business | Oct 2025 | Photonics Spectra
Notes: ELI Beamlines L4 ATON is a high-energy CPA beamline with a 10 PW design goal and a reported multi-PW demonstrated milestone.
Description: The L4 ATON laser at ELI Beamlines is Europe's highest-energy ultrafast laser system and has demonstrated peak powers exceeding 5 PW while targeting 10 PW operation. Its exceptionally high pulse energy makes it a premier platform for relativistic laser–matter interaction, secondary particle generation, and high-energy-density physics experiments.

Institution: LULI / CNRS / CEA / École Polytechnique / Université Paris-Saclay
Reference DOI/link: APOLLON: a multi-PW laser facility (Conference Presentation)
Notes: Apollon is a French multi-PW facility using an OPCPA front end and Ti:sapphire amplification, with 10 PW-class design goals and staged commissioning.
Description: Apollon is France's flagship multi-petawatt laser facility located at the Plateau de Saclay. Designed to reach 10 PW, it supports research in relativistic plasma physics, laser-driven particle acceleration, attosecond science, laboratory astrophysics, and strong-field quantum electrodynamics.

Institution: Institute of Laser Engineering, Osaka University
Rep. rate: Shot on demand for 50 PW stage; earlier stages include 100 Hz and 0.01 Hz concepts
Laser technology: OPCPA using p-DKDP; pumped by DPSSL/split-disk/LFEX-derived pump concepts
Notes: GEKKO-EXA/J-EPoCH is a concept-stage Japanese OPCPA roadmap aiming toward 50 PW, 10 fs pulses using p-DKDP and high-energy pump lasers.
Description: GEKKO-EXA (also referred to as the J-EPoCH concept) is a proposed high-energy laser initiative at Osaka University's Institute of Laser Engineering. It seeks to combine ultrahigh peak power with high repetition rate using advanced diode-pumped architectures, supporting inertial fusion energy, laboratory astrophysics, and advanced laser–plasma accelerator research.
